## Statement
Asymmetry in cortical thickness between hemispheres increases with early neurodegeneration but remains stable in normal aging.

## Mechanism
Neurodegenerative processes preferentially affect one hemisphere, creating measurable asymmetry detectable by structural MRI before volumetric loss is apparent.

## Literature Grounding
- [[hemispheric-asymmetry-in-neurodegeneration]]
- [[structural-mri-early-detection]]

## Testable Predictions
- [ ] Asymmetry index is higher in MCI converters vs stable controls at baseline
- [ ] Asymmetry change rate predicts conversion within 24 months

## Proposed Experiments
Longitudinal structural MRI analysis with FreeSurfer cortical thickness parcellation.

## Assumptions
- Assumption 1: FreeSurfer parcellation has sufficient precision for asymmetry detection -- Status: supported

## Limitations & Risks
Motion artifacts and scanner differences may confound asymmetry measurements.
